🧪 This week’s new distraction (still no Epstein files?) is actually an old one. The claim that acetaminophen (Tylenol™) causes autism has been floating around for decades—and it’s just as much junk science now as it was then. As a father of an autistic child, who is now an adult, I pored over the medical literature. What I found is clear: autism has a strong genetic basis. Most of us likely carry at least one of the potential genes, and environmental triggers may play a role—but acetaminophen isn’t one of them. This drug has been the go-to pain reliever for pregnant women since the 1960s. So if it were the cause, why did autism diagnoses spike in the 1990s and 2000s? The timeline doesn’t add up. And when researchers control for sibling differences—where a mother used acetaminophen during one pregnancy but not another—the autism rates are the same. That points to family factors, not medication. But that won’t stop Robert Kennedy Jr. from pushing anti-science narratives. Under his leadership, HHS is gutting real research in favor of conspiracy theories and fearmongering. Our current senators won’t stand up to this. I will. We need rational voices in Washington—leaders who will fight  …
